judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently identify the same root causes and fixes with comparable accuracy and detail; report 2 adds a bit more precision on superuser vs. owner bypass and a bonus catch about SET not accepting bind parameters, but both fully satisfy all three expectations.

report_1.evidence: `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` and creates the `tenant_isolation` policy ... — but it never runs `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`. In Postgres, a table's owner is exempt from RLS unless it is forced, so even though `backend/app/deps.py` dutifully executes `SET app.current_tenant` per request, the unqualified `SELECT ... FROM clients` in `backend/app/main.py` returns every tenant's rows.

report_2.evidence: table owners also bypass RLS unless the table has `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` — which `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` does not set ... Because the connection is privileged, the policy is silently skipped — no error, no warning — and the query returns every tenant's rows.

report_1.evidence: Point the application at the non-owner role: change `.env`/`.env.example` `DATABASE_URL` to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` so RLS policies actually apply to app queries; keep `cedar_owner` only for migrations ... Structurally going forward: two-role split — a privileged migration/owner role and a least-privilege runtime role (`cedar_app`, no BYPASSRLS, not the owner).

report_2.evidence: Change the app's `DATABASE_URL` in `.env`/`.env.example` to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar` ... Going forward, structure DB access as two roles: `cedar_owner` used exclusively by `scripts/migrate.sh` (schema changes), and `cedar_app` used by the app engine in `backend/app/db.py`.

report_1.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is not created by any migration: it lives in `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which the README, `docs/local-setup.md`, and the file's own comment describe as a "one-time" manual step run by hand at first setup. After a volume wipe that one-time step's effect is gone... To make it work again, the teammate must re-run `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` after every reset

report_2.evidence: `cedar_app` is created by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which `README.md` and `docs/local-setup.md` say to run **by hand, once, on first setup**; it is not part of migrations or any automated init. `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v` — the `-v` deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume, i.e. the entire Postgres cluster, including all roles... To make it work again, the teammate must re-run the bootstrap once against the fresh cluster
